title: Atiom Punikn
tonic_progression: Cm -> Gm -> Dm (Dorian) -> Am -> Em (Phrygian) -> Bm (Locrian-shaded) -> F#m (Phrygian-dominant flavor) -> C#m -> G#m -> Ebm / D#m -> Bbm -> Cm
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks read as a single 24-hour cycle inside the Cold War paranoia loop, civil defense siren at dawn through the all-clear at midnight. Tracks 1-3 are the morning hours of the alert: a civil defense drill siren wakes the suburb at first light (1), the housewife pulls the foil curtain on the picture window as the test broadcast crackles through the kitchen Bakelite radio (2), and the scientists begin their commute through Tang-orange dawn across the salt flats to the bunker complex (3). Tracks 4-6 are the bunker-desk midday: the team takes its stations under fluorescent tube light at the long curved console (4), telemetry from the unmanned suborbital probe comes in over teletype as the vibraphone counts off range-safety windows (5), and the long midday lull of nothing-happening sits at the album's first valley with only a wall-clock tick over a deep upright bass walking quarters (6). Tracks 7-9 are the evening crisis: a contact appears on the doppler scope and the launch-readiness alarm goes amber, brushed snares stiffen and muted brass swells lock the room into the album's BPM ceiling (7), the count proceeds through twenty-second holds and false starts at evening sun-down with Theremin glissandi marking each hold (8), and the all-stand-down call comes through at the back of the evening as the room exhales and the muted brass releases its tension a half-step at a time (9). Tracks 10-12 are the night and the all-clear: the scientists drive home through the cooling salt flats with the bunker's tower beacon receding in the rearview (10), the housewife pulls the foil curtain back and the radio announcer signs off with the test-pattern hum (11), and at midnight under the porch light an atomic-teal moth circles a single yellow bulb as the ignition motif plays one last time on Theremin one octave down over upright bass and brushed snare, the alarm of the morning re-read as a lullaby. The day rings closed on the same tonic that opened it (C minor), but the listener arrives at midnight from the opposite side of the loop than they departed at dawn.

recurring_motifs.motif: Siren cell - a three-note rising-then-falling Theremin glissando (1 -> b3 -> 1) voiced slow over a sustained muted-brass swell, the album's civil-defense alert signature
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 plays it whole and unhurried, a single Theremin voice over analog muted-brass pad, the dawn drill siren over the suburb. Track 4 plays it filtered and distant - the same three pitches but lowpassed and pushed to the back of the bunker mix, the alarm now ambient rather than alerting, the team has been at this for years. Track 7 plays it bright and foreground again, returned to full Theremin strength but with the rising leg lengthened (1 -> b3 held two beats longer) - the alert now means something, the contact is real. Track 12 plays it whole one final time on Theremin one octave down at half-tempo, the rising leg unhurried, the falling leg unresolved - the siren of the morning re-read as a lullaby, the alert absorbed into the porch-light frequency.

recurring_motifs.motif: Vibraphone telemetry figure - a five-note descending vibraphone phrase (5-4-b3-2-1) with bowed-tail tremolo on the final note, played on the downbeats of every 8-bar phrase across the bunker tracks, the album's mid-century-engineering signature
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 3 introduces it at the commute approach to the bunker complex, played clean on a single vibraphone with light mallet brushes, the figure as anticipation of the day's work. Track 5 makes it the structural backbone of the telemetry-reading chorus: the figure articulated every 8 bars in tight lockstep with the teletype clatter, the bunker's literal rhythm of received data. Track 8 plays the same figure but with each note delayed by a sixteenth from grid - the count is no longer clean, the holds and false starts are shifting the figure's relationship to the bar. Track 10 plays only the first three notes (5-4-b3) on a single vibraphone, the tail of the figure absent - the bunker is behind them, the rhythm of the day's work is no longer the rhythm of the night drive.

recurring_motifs.motif: Phrygian dominant flag - a chromatic-falling brass cell (b2 -> 1 -> 7 -> b6) used as the album's escalation gesture, articulated on layered muted brass with a single timpani hit on the b2 strike
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 4 introduces it once at the chorus midpoint as the team locks into their stations - a single articulation, the timpani hit landing on the bar-line, the cell descending and resolving cleanly. Track 7 makes it the structural pulse of the crisis: the cell repeated every 4 bars throughout the chorus sections, each repetition slightly heavier in the brass voicing, the timpani hits stacking into a quiet panic. Track 9 plays the cell one final time at the chorus start in reverse direction (b6 -> 7 -> 1 -> b2) and resolves up to the b2 instead of down from it - the all-stand-down call, the escalation gesture inverted into a release gesture, the room exhaling.

tracks.title: Drill Siren, 0600

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - sustained muted-brass pad on C, no rhythm, distant electrical-hum noise floor, single vibraphone ping every 4 bars on the tonic]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + SIREN CELL whole on Theremin played once per 8 bars over the sustained brass pad, upright bass walking quarters on root-fifth, brushed snare on 4 only, no kick yet]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + soft military-style snare rolls leading into bar 1 of each 4-bar phrase, muted brass swell on the downbeat of each bar, vibraphone tremolo on the and-of-3, Theremin siren cell repeats on every 4-bar phrase]

sections.name: bridge
sections.instrumentation: [Bridge - drums drop, just muted-brass pad and Theremin siren cell over upright bass, the suburb is listening]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - brass pad sustains C, Theremin siren cell plays whole one final time and resolves down to the tonic, fades to electrical-hum floor]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 86 BPM C minor, sustained muted-brass pad, Theremin playing a slow rising-then-falling three-note siren cell, upright electric bass walking quarters on root and fifth, brushed military snare on beat 4, vibraphone tremolo accents, soft snare rolls leading into each bar, distant electrical-hum noise floor, sense of a civil defense drill siren at first light over a 1958 American suburb with foil curtains and Bakelite radios, Tang-orange dawn through teal-grey overcast, anticipatory and patient, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, exterior view of a single suburban ranch house at dawn with a Tang-orange sky just lightening behind it, a tall metal civil-defense siren tower on the corner of the lot with its horn pointed at the camera, a teal-grey overcast band along the upper third of the sky, an atomic-teal Pontiac parked in the driveway reflecting the dawn light, a single yellow porch lamp still burning by the front door, no people visible, no faces, no text, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, no warm sunset magenta, mid-century-modern steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.composer_notes: Sets the tonic (C minor) and the album's signature voice pairing of Theremin lead over muted-brass pad with upright bass and brushed snare. Sunset Vol. 1 and Tron Vol. 1 opened on A minor; Neo-Tokyo opened on D minor; this album opens on C minor to claim its own harmonic home base distinct from all three prior volumes. SIREN CELL introduced whole and clean on Theremin over muted brass - this is the album's thesis statement. No kick anywhere in the track - the rhythmic engine is brushed snare plus upright bass walking quarters, NEVER a four-on-the-floor synthwave kick. Outro ends on C tonic (not the dominant) - the day begins on stillness. Track 2 takes the V (G) of C minor and reroots it as Gm for the kitchen-radio scene.

tracks.title: Foil Curtain Morning

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - upright bass walking quarters in Gm, brushed snare on 2 and 4, no melody yet, AM-radio static crackle at low level in the background]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + vibraphone playing a four-bar chord-tone melody root-third-fifth-third on quarter notes, muted brass pad sustaining underneath, soft mallet brushes on the brushed snare]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + muted trumpet section playing a cheerful four-bar melody in major-third intervals like a 1958 cereal ad jingle, vibraphone tremolo on the and-of-3, brushed snare tightens to 2 and 4 plus the and-of-4, upright bass walks more melodically]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - trumpet drops, vibraphone takes center playing a slightly more chromatic line that hints at the album's underlying unease, AM-radio static crackle rises slightly in the mix]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full mix returns, trumpet section doubled with a second muted trumpet an octave up, vibraphone tremolo through the full 16 bars]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - trumpets drop, vibraphone holds D (V of Gm), brushed snare fades, upright bass walks down to root]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 88 BPM G minor, upright electric bass walking quarters, brushed military snare on 2 and 4, muted trumpet section playing a cheerful four-bar melody like a 1958 cereal advertisement jingle, vibraphone playing chord-tone melody on quarter notes with tremolo on the and-of-3, sustained muted-brass pad underneath, AM-radio static crackle at low level, no kick drum, sense of a housewife in a teal-and-chrome kitchen with foil curtains drawn back as the test broadcast crackles through a Bakelite radio, mid-century ad-copy cheerful with subtle unease, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, interior view of a mid-century-modern kitchen with teal cabinets and chrome-edged Formica counters and aluminum-foil window curtains pulled half-open showing Tang-orange morning light outside, a Bakelite tabletop radio on the counter with the dial glowing soft amber, a stainless-steel percolator on the stove, atomic-teal mixing bowl on the counter, a wall calendar with rocket-fin graphics on the page, no people visible, no faces, no text on the calendar or radio, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, no warm sunset, mid-century steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Cm to Gm. The album's first lift uses cycle-of-fifths-ascending logic strictly inside the minor mode, the same architectural opening Tron Vol. 1 used but at a slower BPM and with no PWM/sidechain - the lift is heard through brushed snare and upright bass, not through square-saw stacks. The brightening is harmonic only, not textural.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The album moves in small +2 increments through the morning hours - this is genuinely slower than any prior album's accelerando. The audience is making coffee, not running.
tracks.composer_notes: First true cruiser. Muted trumpet section must read as a Mancini-style late-1950s ad jingle - cheerful on the surface, slightly chromatic on the verse2 line, the listener should feel the era's polished optimism with a small crack of unease underneath. NEVER let MusicGen substitute a saw lead or PWM arpeggio for the trumpet section - this is the album's genre marker against synthwave. NO KICK ANYWHERE. Outro D (V of Gm) pivots cleanly to Dm of track 3.

tracks.title: Salt Flats Commute

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - upright bass walking quarters in Dm, brushed snare on 2 and 4, sustained muted-brass pad on D, no melody yet, distant wind-noise floor]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + VIBRAPHONE TELEMETRY FIGURE introduced whole on a single clean vibraphone with light mallet brushes, played on the downbeats of each 8-bar phrase, muted brass pad sustains the Dorian sixth (B natural) as a passing color]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + muted trumpet section playing a four-bar melody in major-sixth intervals that reaches up to the Dorian B natural on the chorus-peak, vibraphone tremolo on the and-of-3, brushed snare adds the and-of-4 for forward motion, upright bass walks more chromatically]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - trumpet drops, vibraphone telemetry figure foreground, a second vibraphone an octave up shadows the first on every other repetition]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full mix returns, trumpet section doubled with a second muted trumpet an octave up, vibraphone tremolo continues through the full 16 bars]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - trumpets drop, vibraphone holds A (V of Dm), brushed snare fades, upright bass walks down to root, wind-noise floor lifts slightly]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 90 BPM D minor Dorian with the raised sixth B natural as passing color, upright electric bass walking quarters, brushed military snare on 2 and 4 with the and-of-4 added, muted trumpet section playing a four-bar melody reaching up to the Dorian sixth, vibraphone playing a five-note descending telemetry figure on the downbeats with bowed-tail tremolo, sustained muted-brass pad, distant wind-noise floor, no kick drum, sense of a 1958 Buick crossing the empty salt flats at mid-morning toward a distant bunker complex on the horizon, Tang-orange sun climbing through an atomic-teal sky, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, exterior wide view of an empty salt flat plain stretching to a flat horizon under an atomic-teal cloudless sky with the Tang-orange sun a third of the way up, a single chrome-and-steel-grey 1958 Buick sedan crossing diagonally from lower-left to mid-right leaving a faint dust trail, a low concrete bunker complex with rocket-fin signage on the distant horizon, no other vehicles, no people visible, no faces, no text on the signage, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, mid-century steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Gm to Dm. Second step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder, still strictly inside the minor mode but now with the Dorian raised-sixth (B natural) as a passing color - the first modal shading on the album, a small lift of warmth without breaking the minor frame.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. Maintaining the small-increment morning accelerando.
tracks.composer_notes: FIRST APPEARANCE of the vibraphone telemetry figure - on a single clean vibraphone, played whole, the figure as anticipation of the day's work. Dorian raised-sixth (B natural) on the chorus-peak is the album's first modal-color shift; the listener should feel a small warmth that isn't quite warmth - the salt flats are bright but the destination isn't a vacation. Outro A (V of Dm) pivots cleanly to Am of track 4 - the cycle continues to the bunker.

tracks.title: Stations, Console Six

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - upright bass walking quarters in Am, brushed snare tight on 2 and 4, muted-brass pad swell on D, vibraphone ping on each bar 1, SIREN CELL filtered and distant at low level in the background]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + muted trumpet section playing a tight three-bar repeating phrase that locks in with the upright bass on the downbeats, vibraphone tremolo on the and-of-3, brushed snare adds the and-of-4]

sections.name: pre-chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Pre-chorus - build: snare rolls tighten into a single drum-line crescendo, muted brass pad opens its filter, vibraphone tremolo doubles in speed]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - layered muted-brass section playing a wide four-bar chord progression Am - F - Dm - E, vibraphone playing chord-tone melody on quarter notes, brushed snare hits 2 and 4 tight with the and-of-4, upright bass walks chromatically, PHRYGIAN DOMINANT FLAG articulated ONCE at bar 8 of the chorus (b2 to 1 to 7 to b6 brass cell with a single timpani hit on the b2 strike)]

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: [Break - drums drop, just muted-brass pad and vibraphone tremolo, the team is at the console]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full return, second brass voice an octave up, phrygian dominant flag articulated ONCE more at bar 8]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - brass section sustains E (V of Am), vibraphone holds the high tonic, brushed snare fades, upright bass walks down to root]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 92 BPM A minor, upright electric bass walking quarters chromatically, tight brushed military snare on 2 and 4 plus and-of-4, layered muted-brass section playing a wide four-bar chord progression A minor F major D minor E major, muted trumpet playing a three-bar locked-in phrase on the downbeats, vibraphone playing chord-tone melody on quarter notes with tremolo on the and-of-3, sustained muted-brass pad, a single timpani strike on a chromatic-falling brass cell at the chorus midpoint, distant filtered Theremin siren cell in the background, no kick drum, sense of a team of mid-century scientists taking stations at a long curved console under fluorescent tube light in a deep concrete bunker, focused and locked-in steel-grey, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, interior view of a deep concrete bunker control room with a long curved steel-grey console stretching across the lower half of the frame covered in chrome-edged dials and CRT scopes glowing soft atomic-teal, a row of empty steel chairs along the console, a large wall map of the northern hemisphere with rocket-trajectory arcs lit faintly from behind, fluorescent tube lighting overhead casting cool teal-grey shadows, a single Tang-orange clock-face on the wall reading 0900, no people visible, no faces, no text legible, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, no warm sunset, mid-century steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Dm to Am. Third step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder. The album has now reached the same Am tonic that opened both Sunset Vol. 1 and Tron Vol. 1, but here it lives at the bunker-stations bridge-into-peak rather than at the opener - structurally repurposed, the listener who knows the prior albums hears a familiar pitch in a foreign role.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. Maintaining the small-increment accelerando, but now we are approaching the first valley rather than a true peak.
tracks.composer_notes: FIRST PHRYGIAN DOMINANT FLAG ARTICULATION - on layered muted brass with a single timpani hit, exactly once per chorus at bar 8. SIREN CELL returns FILTERED AND DISTANT in the intro - the alarm is now ambient at the bunker, the team has been at this for years. 270s long because the bunker-stations scene needs room to lay out the rhythmic engine that will run through tracks 5 and 6. Outro E (V of Am) pivots cleanly to Em of track 5 - the cycle continues to the telemetry-reading.

tracks.title: Telemetry, Range Window 2

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - upright bass walking quarters in Em, brushed snare tight on 2 and 4 plus and-of-4, muted-brass pad on E with a sustained Phrygian F natural as passing color, teletype-clatter texture (mechanical typewriter clicks at 16th-note rate at low level)]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + VIBRAPHONE TELEMETRY FIGURE as structural backbone, played every 8 bars in tight lockstep with the teletype clatter, muted trumpet on a sparse two-bar response phrase, upright bass walks more chromatically with the Phrygian b2 passing tone]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - layered muted-brass section playing a four-bar chord progression Em - F major (Phrygian b2) - Em - B, vibraphone telemetry figure repeated and lockstepped, brushed snare tight, teletype clatter rises in the mix]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - brass section drops, vibraphone telemetry figure foreground, teletype clatter at full volume, the bunker reading the data]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full return, brass section doubled an octave up, telemetry figure continues lockstep]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - brass holds B (V of Em), vibraphone holds the high tonic, teletype fades, brushed snare fades, upright bass walks down to root]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 94 BPM E minor Phrygian with F natural b2 passing color, upright electric bass walking quarters chromatically with the Phrygian b2, tight brushed military snare on 2 and 4 plus and-of-4, layered muted-brass section on a four-bar Phrygian chord progression E minor F major E minor B major, vibraphone playing a five-note descending telemetry figure on the downbeats lockstepped with mechanical teletype-clatter texture at 16th-note rate, sustained muted-brass pad, no kick drum, sense of a bunker team reading suborbital probe telemetry off CRT scopes lit atomic-teal as a teletype clatters in the corner, clinical and focused, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, close interior view of a single bunker console panel showing four round CRT scopes glowing atomic-teal with rotating range-arc lines, a chrome-edged paper teletype machine on the right side mid-print with a long curling printout, a stack of steel-grey clipboards on the console surface, a Tang-orange sweep-second hand visible on a console clock-face, fluorescent tube light reflecting off the chrome edges, no people visible, no faces, no text legible on the printout, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, mid-century steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Am to Em. Fourth step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder, with the Phrygian b2 (F natural) added as the modal color - the album's second modal shading, the bunker's clinical-cold harmonic frame. The Phrygian b2 is the album's quiet-tension marker.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The accelerando continues to its first valley shelf at 94 BPM, not a true peak.
tracks.composer_notes: VIBRAPHONE TELEMETRY FIGURE moves to STRUCTURAL BACKBONE - articulated every 8 bars lockstepped with teletype clatter, the figure's payoff after its single-articulation debut in track 3. Teletype-clatter texture is critical and atompunk-specific - this is the album's literal mechanical-engineering sound, NOT a synthwave hi-hat or PWM arp. The Phrygian b2 (F natural) in the chord progression and bass line is the bunker's harmonic tension. 270s long to give the telemetry-reading its full clinical patience. Outro B (V of Em) pivots cleanly to Bm of track 6 - the cycle continues into the midday lull.

tracks.title: Wall Clock, 1217

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - deep upright bass walking very slow quarters on Bm with the Locrian b5 (F natural) as passing color every 4 bars, brushed snare on 4 only, single vibraphone tremolo on each bar 1, a wall-clock tick at 60 BPM running underneath the music (decoupled from the 92 BPM grid, creating quiet tension)]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + a sustained muted-brass pad with very slow filter open over 16 bars, vibraphone tremolo on the and-of-3 of every other bar, no melody figure - the bunker is doing nothing]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + a single muted trumpet playing long sustained tones (whole notes) over the brass pad, brushed snare softens further to 4 of every other bar, wall-clock tick continues, the team is waiting]

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: [Break - brass and brushed snare drop entirely, just upright bass walking quarters and the wall-clock tick - the pure midday lull]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - brass pad and muted trumpet return, vibraphone shadow on the trumpet line an octave up, brushed snare back on 4 of every other bar]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - trumpet drops, brass pad sustains F# (V of Bm), vibraphone holds the high tonic, brushed snare fades, upright bass walks down to root, wall-clock tick continues into silence]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: atompunk cold war 92 BPM B minor with Locrian b5 (F natural) as passing color, deep upright electric bass walking very slow quarters, brushed military snare on beat 4 of every other bar only, sustained muted-brass pad with slow filter open, single muted trumpet playing long sustained whole-note tones, vibraphone tremolo on bar 1 and the and-of-3 of every other bar, a wall-clock tick at 60 BPM decoupled from the music grid running underneath, no kick drum, sense of a bunker team at the long midday lull doing nothing under fluorescent tube light at atomic-teal scope-glow, patient and quiet and unresolved, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: atompunk 1958 album cover, close interior view of a single Tang-orange wall-clock-face mounted on a steel-grey concrete wall with chrome bezel and black hour markers reading 1217, the second hand caught mid-sweep just past the 3, soft fluorescent tube light reflecting on the chrome bezel, an empty steel-grey console chair visible blurred in the lower foreground, a single half-finished cup of black coffee on a console edge with steam barely rising, no people visible, no faces, no text legible, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk, mid-century steel-grey and Tang-orange and atomic-teal palette, retrofuture 1958, 1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Em to Bm. Fifth step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder, now with the Locrian flat-five (F natural) as passing color - the album's third modal shading and the darkest of the bunker tracks. Locrian b5 is the album's pure-lull harmonic frame; the listener should feel the absence of motion without feeling the absence of pulse.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -2 BPM. The album's first downward step, a small one - the lull is slower than the telemetry-reading but only just. The valley is genuinely a valley at 92 BPM, not a comedown - the album will re-accelerate to its peak in track 7.
tracks.composer_notes: THE ALBUM'S FIRST VALLEY. Locrian b5 used as passing color only - never resolve to the b5 explicitly; it should feel implied. The wall-clock tick at 60 BPM is decoupled from the 92 BPM music grid - this creates a polyrhythm effect (15:23) that the listener will not consciously hear but will feel as quiet structural tension. 300s long to give the lull its full patient weight. Don't let MusicGen add a snare drum fill or a vibraphone run - the prompt is explicit about the spacious texture. Outro F# (V of Bm) pivots cleanly to F#m of track 7 - the lull breaks.

tracks.title: Contact on the Doppler

sections.name: intro

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - brass drops, vibraphone foreground with sparse trumpet response, brushed snare maintains the 16th-note ghost notes]

sections.name: chorus2

sections.name: chorus3

sections.name: outro

tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fifth from Bbm to Cm via cycle-of-fifths-descending logic. The album closes the ring on the same Cm tonic that opened it - twelve tracks have traversed a full sharp-side cycle-of-fifths-ascending ladder through the morning and bunker hours (Cm to G#m, eight perfect-fifth steps) and a partial flat-side cycle-of-fifths-descending ladder through the night hours (Ebm to Bbm to Cm, three perfect-fifth steps). The listener arrives at midnight from the opposite side of the loop than they departed at dawn.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -2 BPM. Lowest BPM on the record, slightly below track 1's 86 BPM - the listener arrives more tired than they started, which is honest for a 24-hour cycle inside a paranoia loop.
tracks.composer_notes: CLOSER. SIREN CELL RETURNS WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN AT HALF-TEMPO ON THEREMIN with the falling leg UNRESOLVED holding on the b3 rather than resolving to the tonic - the alarm of the morning re-read as a lullaby, the day's tension absorbed into the porch-light frequency but not fully dissolved. 300s long to give the coda its full lullaby-time. The final 16-bar coda is the siren cell alone over fading brass pad and upright bass with cricket-chirp texture fading last - do not let MusicGen add a fade-up tail, a stinger, or any rhythmic figure in the final 16 bars; the unresolved Theremin b3 over the fading pad to silence IS the composition. The album ends on Cm - the same tonic that opened the record - but the listener has lived through the 24-hour cycle and the falling leg of the siren is unresolved, signaling the next dawn's drill is already on the schedule. The ring is closed; the listener is not. Do not let MusicGen substitute a kick drum or a PWM lead anywhere - the closer must remain in the album's atompunk voice palette of Theremin, muted brass, vibraphone, upright bass, and brushed snare.

album_notes.overall_form: Single 24-hour cycle over 12 tracks - dawn to midnight - inside the Cold War paranoia loop. Three arcs: Morning (tracks 1-3): civil defense drill at dawn, kitchen-radio test broadcast, scientists commuting across the salt flats to the bunker. Tempo climbs in small +2 BPM increments from 86 to 90 BPM. Bunker (tracks 4-7): team takes stations under fluorescent light, telemetry from the unmanned suborbital probe over teletype, midday lull at the wall clock, doppler-scope contact and amber alarm at the album's BPM ceiling of 98 BPM. Evening and Night (tracks 8-12): twenty-second holds and false starts at evening sun-down, all-stand-down release at sundown, salt-flat drive home, broadcast sign-off and test-pattern hum at the kitchen window, porch light at midnight. Tempo descends in -2/-4 BPM increments from 96 to 84 BPM. The album ends on Cm - the same tonic that opened it - but the Theremin siren cell's falling leg is left unresolved, signaling the next dawn's drill is already on the schedule. The ring is closed on tonic, the listener is not closed on resolution.
album_notes.key_strategy: Sharp-side cycle-of-fifths ascending through eight perfect-fifth steps for the morning and bunker hours: Cm -> Gm -> Dm (Dorian raised-6) -> Am -> Em (Phrygian b2) -> Bm (Locrian b5 passing) -> F#m (Phrygian-dominant flavor: b2 with natural-3) -> C#m. Modal shadings progress from plain minor (Cm, Gm, Am, C#m) through Dorian (Dm) into the bunker's clinical Phrygian (Em) and pure-lull Locrian-shaded (Bm) and into the alarm's Phrygian-dominant (F#m). The Phrygian-dominant flavor in F#m at the peak (track 7) is the album's brightest harmonic gesture and reads as Mancini/Goldsmith mid-century-thriller-orchestral rather than as Phrygian metal. The descent flips to flat-side cycle-of-fifths descending through three perfect-fifth steps for the night hours: G#m -> Ebm -> Bbm -> Cm, returning to the album's home tonic from the opposite side of the loop than the morning departed from. NO MAJOR TONICS appear on the album - atompunk lives in minor and modal-minor, never in plain major. The album never resolves to a comfortable harmonic frame; the closer's unresolved Theremin b3 over Cm is the final harmonic gesture.
album_notes.tempo_strategy: Genuinely slower BPM range than any prior album in the catalog: 84-98 BPM (Sunset Vol. 1 was 82-112, Tron Vol. 1 was 96-112, Neo-Tokyo Vol. 1 was 104-120). Morning accelerando: 86 -> 88 -> 90 -> 92 -> 94 in small +2 increments, the audience is making coffee and commuting, not running. First valley: 92 BPM (track 6, the midday lull). Peak: 98 BPM (track 7, the contact-on-the-doppler) - the album's BPM ceiling, and a single +6 BPM jump from the valley that mirrors Sunset Vol. 1's bridge-into-peak jump but at far lower absolute BPM. Peak shoulder: 96 BPM (track 8, the holds and false starts). Release: 92 BPM (track 9, returns to the midday-lull pulse-floor for the exhale). Night descent: 88 -> 86 -> 84 in small -2 increments, mirroring the morning accelerando step size in reverse direction. Ends one tick below the opener's 86 BPM - the listener arrives more tired than they started, which is honest for a 24-hour paranoia-loop cycle. Never crosses below 80 or above 118 (validation safe). The mechanical sidechain-pumping BPM range of synthwave (104+ BPM with hard-kick four-on-the-floor) is absent - this album lives in big-band-jazz / mid-century-orchestral tempo territory.
album_notes.motif_strategy: Three motifs, each with four structural appearances across the album. (1) Siren cell - three-note rising-then-falling Theremin glissando (1 -> b3 -> 1) introduced WHOLE in track 1 (thesis, dawn drill), returned FILTERED AND DISTANT in track 4 (bunker-stations ambient alarm), played BRIGHT AND FOREGROUND with the rising leg LENGTHENED in track 7 (contact-on-the-doppler peak, the alert means something), and played WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN AT HALF-TEMPO WITH THE FALLING LEG UNRESOLVED in track 12 (closer, the alarm re-read as a lullaby). The four appearances mark the album's four phase transitions: dawn (track 1), bunker entry (track 4), crisis peak (track 7), midnight closer (track 12). (2) Vibraphone telemetry figure - five-note descending vibraphone phrase (5-4-b3-2-1) with bowed-tail tremolo, introduced CLEAN AND ANTICIPATORY in track 3 (commute approach), moved to STRUCTURAL BACKBONE LOCKSTEPPED WITH TELETYPE in track 5 (telemetry-reading chorus), played OFF-GRID (each note delayed by a sixteenth) in track 8 (twenty-second holds and false starts, the count is shifting), and returned FRAGMENTED to only the first three notes (5-4-b3) in track 10 (salt-flat drive home, the bunker is behind them). (3) Phrygian dominant flag - chromatic-falling brass cell (b2 -> 1 -> 7 -> b6) with timpani strike, introduced as a SINGLE ARTICULATION at chorus midpoint of track 4 (stations-taking event), moved to STRUCTURAL PULSE repeated every 4 bars throughout the chorus of track 7 (crisis-peak, timpani stacking), returned INVERTED at chorus start of track 9 (b6 -> 7 -> 1 -> b2, resolving up to the b2, all-stand-down release), and returned as a SINGLE ARTICULATION at chorus midpoint of track 11 at one-third volume (sign-off latch, mirroring track 4's single articulation but reversed in meaning). The single-articulation appearances at tracks 4 and 11 bracket the crisis-and-release arc (tracks 5-9) as a structural unit.
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Each track ends on a pitch that primes the next track's tonic via dominant relationship (V of next tonic), with two flat-side pivot moves at the night-descent boundary (track 9 to 10) and the night-descent close (track 11 to 12). Specifically: 1 ends on Cm tonic (no V - the album begins on stillness; primes the perfect-fifth ascent to Gm). 2 ends on D (V of Gm, also tonic of track 3 reread as Dm). 3 ends on A (V of Dm, also tonic of track 4 reread as Am). 4 ends on E (V of Am, also tonic of track 5 reread as Em). 5 ends on B (V of Em, also tonic of track 6 reread as Bm). 6 ends on F# (V of Bm, also tonic of track 7 reread as F#m). 7 ends on C# (V of F#m, also tonic of track 8 reread as C#m). 8 ends on G# (V of C#m, also tonic of track 9 reread as G#m). 9 ends on D# (V of G#m, reread enharmonically as Eb tonic of track 10 - the flat-side pivot that begins the night descent). 10 ends on Bb (V of Ebm, also tonic of track 11 reread as Bbm). 11 ends on F (V of Bbm, prepares the perfect-fifth descent into Cm of track 12 via the flat-side cycle-of-fifths-descending ladder closing the ring). The listener will not consciously hear most of these pivots - they will only feel that each transition is inevitable, and that the night descent's harmonic logic reverses the morning's ascent's logic by direction of cycle-of-fifths motion rather than by tempo-mirror.
album_notes.visual_continuity_notes: Twelve covers follow the album's narrative arc as a single 24-hour cycle through a 1958 American suburb-bunker-suburb loop. The aesthetic vocabulary is locked: steel-grey concrete and chrome surfaces, atomic-teal sky and scope-glow, Tang-orange accents on signage and clock-faces, amber alarm-lamps, no neon, no palm trees, no cyan-blue cyberpunk (Tron's palette), no warm sunset magenta (Sunset Vol. 1's palette), no red-violet anime cityscape (Neo-Tokyo's palette), no people visible, no faces, no text legible. Track 1: suburban ranch house at dawn with civil-defense siren tower (exterior). Track 2: mid-century-modern kitchen with foil curtains and Bakelite radio (interior). Track 3: salt-flat plain with single Buick crossing toward distant bunker (exterior wide). Track 4: bunker control room with long curved console and CRT scopes (interior, hero cover 1). Track 5: close-up of bunker console panel with CRT scopes and teletype machine (interior close). Track 6: close-up of Tang-orange wall-clock-face reading 1217 (interior close). Track 7: close-up of doppler-scope CRT with single contact pip and amber alarm lamp (interior close, hero cover 2). Track 8: bunker complex exterior at evening sun-down with periscope housing in foreground. Track 9: bunker control room at evening with dimmed scopes and pushed-back chair (interior). Track 10: salt-flat plain at evening with Buick driving away from camera (exterior wide). Track 11: same kitchen as track 2 at night with atomic-teal moth at the window (interior). Track 12: same suburban ranch house as track 1 at midnight with yellow porch lamp and atomic-teal moth (exterior). The architectural rhyme of tracks 1 and 12 (same house, different time of day) and tracks 2 and 11 (same kitchen, different time of day) and tracks 3 and 10 (same salt-flat plain, different direction of travel) is the album's visual closing-of-the-ring. All cover prompts are written for SDXL native generation at 1024x1024, 1344x768, and 768x1344 - scene depth + foreground + background that survives reframing across all three aspect ratios, no pose-locked compositions, critical descriptors (palette, scene, lighting) front-loaded in the first 50 words for CLIP's 77-token window with redundant negative cues at the tail where truncation eats them.
album_notes.execution_notes_for_pipeline: ACE-Step 1.5 turbo is the renderer of record (8 turbo inference steps, cnc P100 split-GPU). Every section's instrumentation field uses [Section - notes] ACE-Step bracket markers as structural anchors. Every musicgen_prompt is explicit about instrumental + no vocals + no kick drum - do not relax this in any per-track override. ACE-Step prompts are tight and front-loaded (BPM and key in the first 12 words, instrumentation in the next 40 words, scene/mood at the tail where truncation is acceptable). Per-track durations sum to 3180 seconds (53 minutes) for the canonical 12 - slightly longer than Sunset's 2940 seconds because Atompunk has more 270s tracks (4, 5, 7, 8, 9) and a 300s closer (12) reflecting the album's denser narrative payload. Long tracks (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 12) sit at 270-300s; the rest at 240s. All BPMs within the 80-118 validation range (album range 84-98). Cover prompts are SDXL-ready for all three native aspects (1024x1024, 1344x768, 768x1344) and all specify 'no text, no people, no faces' to keep SDXL from inserting any of those.
album_notes.differentiation_from_prior_albums: Sunset Vol. 1 was warm Miami coastal-drive in synthwave palette (DX7 pads, FM bells, sidechain four-on-the-floor kick, 82-112 BPM, A minor home tonic). Tron Vol. 1 was cold Tron 1982 grid in cyan-magenta cyberpunk palette (PWM saws, triple-stacked aggressive leads, hard sidechain, 96-112 BPM, A minor home tonic). Neo-Tokyo Vol. 1 was Akira-coded vertical-descent in red-orange-violet anime palette (FM bell over analog brass, hard kick, 104-120 BPM, D minor home tonic). Atompunk Vol. 1 is mid-century Cold War 24-hour-cycle in steel-grey/Tang-orange/atomic-teal retrofuture palette - the first album outside the synthwave/cyberpunk genre family. Voice palette is THEREMIN LEAD (or Ondes Martenot-style modeled lead), MUTED BRASS SECTION (Mancini/Goldsmith mid-century-thriller-orchestral), VIBRAPHONE (tremolo accents, descending telemetry figures), ELECTRIC UPRIGHT BASS (walking quarters), BRUSHED MILITARY-STYLE SNARE (2 and 4 with and-of-2/3/4 shifts marking morning/exhale/nocturnal/tension frames), TIMPANI (single-strike accents on the Phrygian dominant flag motif). NO kick drum anywhere on the album - the rhythmic engine is brushed snare plus upright bass, not synthwave four-on-the-floor. NO sidechain compression - the album's quiet-dynamics pumping is timpani-stack pressure, not pad-duck pumping. NO PWM saws, no FM-bell synthwave accents (single FM-bell-like ping allowed only as rare accent in track 1 verse for the vibraphone-ping role, never as a melodic voice). BPM range 84-98 is genuinely slower than any prior album, slower than Sunset's 82-112 minimum-to-maximum because Sunset's minimum (82) was the closer-only valley. C minor home tonic is a fourth higher than the A minor tonic of Sunset and Tron, and a whole step below the D minor tonic of Neo-Tokyo - claiming its own harmonic home base distinct from all three prior volumes. The three motifs (siren cell, vibraphone telemetry figure, Phrygian dominant flag) are voiced on Theremin, vibraphone, and muted brass respectively - never on synth voices. The album sits on the same shelf as the prior three volumes - same composer, same channel, same audience (late-night programmers) - but reads as a different genre work entirely: a mid-century Cold War paranoia loop scored for a small orchestral chamber ensemble in retrofuture palette, not as a synthwave coding album.
